finding: Rigveda saṃhitā text is highly stable across the TWO independently-collatable witness families (Aufrecht 1863 / F-AUFRECHT, and Lubotsky / F-LUBOTSKY).
method: 10,552 CUIDs built; each aligned at verse+pada level; comparison after accent-strip, whitespace/danda removal, sandhi-final collapse, and sandhi-junction 'a' removal.
interpretation: Residual differences are overwhelmingly at WORD BOUNDARIES (external sandhi: o vs a+ḥ, r vs ḥ, tvagne vs tva+agne, parehi vs para+i) and ACCENT PLACEMENT — i.e., editorial/orthographic/segmentation convention, not lexical variants. A full Vedic sandhi engine would resolve nearly all 'candidate' cases. Lexical divergence between the two independent bases is rare.
falsification_of_prior_claim: The earlier '3 independent witnesses / remarkably stable' claim was PARTIALLY REJECTED (VNH is Aufrecht-derived, not independent), then RE-TESTED on the corrected 2-base set. The corrected, evidence-backed conclusion: stability holds at the level of *lexical content* across 2 independent families; it is NOT yet certified as 'complete/stable' because only 2 of the required >=3 independent bases are collatable.
remaining_gaps_to_certify: Acquire >=1 more independent saṃhitā base (clean Max Müller OCR or native text; Oldenberg-Schmidt apparatus) and re-collate.
Resolve the 90 Aufrecht-absent verses (likely Khila/appendix numbering; confirm against VedaWeb + Padapāṭha).
Replace the heuristic sandhi normalizer with a proper Vedic sandhi resolver to confirm zero lexical variants.
Acquire native-Unicode Sāyaṇa + Bāṣkala-padapāṭha for recensional coverage.
verdict: EVIDENCE INCOMPLETE (quality gate not met). Baseline CUID set is evidence-backed at the 2-independent-family level and is falsifiable; it is NOT a certified Digital Critical Edition.
